tutorial.drydock_hint: FIRST VOYAGE - Select a charter below to open the PREP screen and ready the ship.
tutorial.drydock_refit_hint: Repair or refit on the SHIP screen, then choose the next charter:

steps.label: Choose a charter
steps.tip: Compare length and reward, then SELECT a charter to consider it. Nothing is committed until you press LAUNCH.

steps.label: Stock food for the voyage
steps.tip: The larder must cover centuries: need = crew x rations x years. The farms offset some of it every year, but a shortfall means starvation in deep space. Use the stock-up button here, or trade on the MARKET screen.

steps.label: Rack spare parts
steps.tip: Upkeep consumes parts every year, and without them the hull wears at full rate. Stock the shortfall before departure - the black sells nothing.

steps.label: Top off the fuel tanks
steps.tip: Fuel burns in transit. A dry tank stalls the voyage and strains every system aboard.

steps.label: Press [ LAUNCH ]
steps.tip: Departure locks the loadout. From here, generations will live and die before the ship sees home again.

obituary: {name} was laid to rest among the stars.
{name} died in the deep, and the ship kept a minute of dark in their name.
{name} was given to the recyclers with full honors; nothing aboard is ever truly lost.
The bells rang once for {name}, who had never known a sky.
{name} passed in their sleep, older than anyone left who remembered the launch.
They committed {name} to the void through the aft lock, wrapped in the mission colors.
succession: {name} took up the founding charter and the weight that comes with it.
The council confirmed {name} as head of the line, hand on the old ship's log.
{name} inherited the name, the duty, and the long dark ahead.
By quiet consent the dynasty turned to {name} to lead it onward.
{name} stepped into the first chair, younger than the post deserved.
coming_of_age: Generation {generation} came of age: {births} new dynasty member(s).
Generation {generation} took their places on the decks — {births} of them, born to the voyage.
The nurseries emptied into the corridors: {births} children of Generation {generation} grown.
{births} new hands joined the work as Generation {generation} came into its own.
Generation {generation} numbered {births} strong, a cohort who would never see the world that sent them.
retirement: {name} stood down from their post, the work passed to a steadier pair of hands.
{name} kept the post a lifetime and gave it up gracefully to the young.
{name} handed over the watch and went to the quiet decks to rest.
{name}'s post found a new holder; the old one had long since earned the ease.
{name} stepped off the duty roster, one more founder's craft handed down the line.
{name} left the post to a former apprentice and did not once look back.
extinction: The dynasty found no heir. The founders' line ends here, in the dark between stars, its long charter unfinished.
The last of the line was laid to rest with no one to follow. What the founders began ends, quietly, aboard the ship they launched.
No heir came. The name that cast the ship off is the name that ends with it, somewhere the charts do not reach.
famine: Rations ran out. The population diminished by {losses}.
Another year with the larder empty; hunger took {losses} more before the count was done.
The galley served what it did not have. {losses} did not see the next harvest.
The ship ate its margins and then its people: {losses} lost to the thin year.
The rationing failed the arithmetic again. {losses} names went to the memorial wall, hunger-thin.
fuel_stall: The tanks ran dry in transit — the ship coasted, and its systems strained in the cold.
Another year adrift on empty tanks; the ship fell further behind its own schedule, coasting and cold.
No burn this year. The ship drifted on momentum alone while its systems wore at the no-fuel rate.
The dry tanks held the ship becalmed a year longer, every deck a degree colder than the last.
appointment: {name} took up the post of {post}, the roster whole again for a while.
{name} was sworn in as {post} — a young hand on an old duty.
The {post}'s watch passed to {name}, who had trained a lifetime for the vacancy.
{name} stepped into the {post}'s post, the founders' craft handed one more link down the chain.
The ship named {name} its {post}; the post had been cold too long.
{name} accepted the {post}'s duties, another berth on the roster filled from the ship's own children.
training: {name}, the {post}, finished the program and came back sharper (skill {skill}).
The ship drilled {name} deeper into the {post}'s craft — skill {skill} and still climbing.
{name} put in the long hours and rose to skill {skill} at the {post}'s post.
Advanced training closed for {name}; the {post}'s hand is steadier now, skill {skill}.
faction_souring: A coldness has settled over {name}; they keep their own counsel now, and their grievances to themselves, which is worse.
Word runs the decks that {name} have grown restless — a people that no longer troubles to petition, having learned what petitioning is worth.
{name} have stopped arguing in council. Those who know the ship's moods find that quiet more worrying than any shouting.
There is a hardness in {name} that was not there a generation ago; they do their work and share no more of themselves than the work requires.
The old warmth between the ship and {name} has gone thin. They stay because they are aboard, not because they are held.
faction_warming: {name} have taken to speaking of the ship as theirs again — a small word, and a telling one.
There is an ease in {name} of late; they give more than the work asks, the way a people does when it feels itself valued.
Word runs the decks that {name} count themselves well-used by this ship, and a people that feels seen is a steadier people to sail with.
{name} have begun keeping the ship's festivals as if they were their own, which, increasingly, they are.
Something has warmed between the ship and {name}; they answer a hard year now the way kin do, without being asked twice.
ship_mood_darkening: A heaviness has settled on the ship of late. Folk keep to their own decks, the corridors have gone quiet in a way that is not rest, and the old songs are sung less and lower.
There is a greyness in the crew this year that no roster change explains — a whole ship carrying its mornings a little harder, laughing a little less, the light in the common decks somehow thinner.
The mood aboard has turned. It shows in small things: shorter tempers at the mess, longer silences on the night watch, a generation that has stopped believing the good years are coming back.
Something has gone out of the ship's spirit. The work still gets done, but joylessly, the way a tired people does a thing because stopping is worse — and everyone aboard can feel the weight of it.
A low season has come over the decks. No single grief accounts for it; it is the sum of a hard stretch settling into the bones of the crew, the ship itself seeming to sail heavier for it.
ship_mood_lifting: A lightness has come over the ship of late. The common decks are loud again in the good way, the festivals better attended, the crew carrying itself like a people that expects tomorrow to be worth showing up for.

Something has lifted in the ship's spirit this year. Folk linger over the shared meals, the corridors ring with the small noise of contentment, and even the hard watches are stood with a lighter heart.
The mood aboard has turned bright. It shows in the singing that starts up unbidden, the children given room to be loud, a whole ship remembering that the long voyage can also be a life well lived.

A warmth has spread through the decks that no single feast explains — the accumulated weight of good years, felt at last as ease, the crew moving through its days gladder than it has been in a generation.

The ship is happy this season, plainly and without apology. The work goes quicker for it, quarrels settle before they harden, and the founders, could they see it, would know their gamble had bought their heirs some joy.
polity_souring: A sourness has crept into the ship's politics of late. It is not one people aggrieved but most of them at once — a general, low-burning sense across the decks that the ship no longer looks after its own, that whoever is deciding things is not deciding them for us.

The peoples of the ship have grown restive together this season. The council chambers are louder and the corridors quieter, and there is a widely shared, rarely spoken conviction that the arrangement everyone lives under has stopped being fair.

Something has curdled in how the ship's peoples regard their governing. No single faction has raised a banner; it is subtler and worse than that — a broad, mutual withdrawal of goodwill, a whole polity deciding, quietly and separately, that it is owed better than it is getting.

A season of grievance has settled over the ship, and it belongs to everyone. The old accommodations between the peoples fray at once, each convinced the others are favored, none convinced the ship is being run for any of them.

The ship's politics have gone cold. Not to open quarrel — to something more corrosive: a general disaffection, peoples that no longer bother to feel aggrieved separately because they have all quietly agreed the whole ship is being mishandled.
polity_warming: A rare ease has come over the ship's politics this season. The peoples, on the whole, feel themselves fairly dealt with — a broad, unshowy contentment that a ship earns only when most of its factions, most of the time, believe they are being looked after.

The old frictions between the peoples have gone quiet together. It is not that the differences are gone, only that a general goodwill has risen over them, a shared and mostly unspoken sense that the ship, this season, is being run for all of them.

Something has settled between the ship's peoples of late. The council works, the accommodations hold, and there is across the decks a widely felt fairness — the quiet civic weather of a polity that trusts, for now, that it is in good hands.

The ship's politics are warm this year in the way that asks no attention: no crisis averted, no grievance answered, just a broad and steady contentment among the peoples, each grudgingly satisfied the others are not being favored over them.

A season of civic ease has come to the ship. The peoples deal with one another and with the council in good faith, a whole polity relaxed for once into the rare belief that the ship is fair, and being run for the good of all who live on it.
reputation_merciful: It comes up in the corridors now, half-joking and half-proud: that the ship is getting a name for itself out in the dark, and the name is a kind one. The hands who deal with passing hulls report a new warmth in how the ship is met — the small courtesy strangers extend to a vessel they have heard good things about.

The ship has begun, without quite deciding to, to be known as a helping hull. It shows in little things — a stranger who hails with unusual trust, a waystation that deals a shade more generously — and the crew wears the growing reputation the way one wears a coat a size too large: a little self-consciously, and glad of the warmth.

Word has gone out, the way word does, that this is a ship that does the decent thing. The crew did not set out to earn the name and cannot quite say when it arrived, but it is there now in the manner of every hull they meet, and there is a quiet pride in the decks that was not there before.
reputation_feared: The hands who deal with passing hulls have started to notice it, and to keep it to themselves: that the ship is getting a name in the dark, and the name is not a warm one. Strangers hail warily now, deal minimally, keep the exits clear — the wide berth one gives a vessel one has heard hard things about.

The ship has begun, one cold choice at a time, to be known as a hull that does the hard thing. It shows in how it is met — a new caution in every hail, a waystation's quiet, a trader who names a fair price fast and does not linger — and the crew notices the change in the dark's manner and does not entirely dislike the room it makes.

Word has gone out that this is a ship best not crossed and best not counted on. The crew did not set out to earn the name, but it is theirs now, plain in the wariness of every hull they meet, and there is a hard set to the decks that was not there before — a people growing, quietly, into the fear it inspires.
stability_fraying: The machinery of the ship's government has begun, quietly, to slip its gears. A council meets and cannot make quorum; an office falls vacant and no one is quite appointed to fill it; a decision that ought to take a week takes a season, or takes no one at all. Nothing has collapsed — it is only that the government is a little less a government than it was.

It is a small thing at first and then it is everywhere: the charter cited and then worked around, the standing orders honored in the letter and quietly ignored in the fact, the offices of the ship going through motions whose reason no one now insists upon. The institutions are fraying — not with a crisis but with a shrug.

The ship's councils have grown slow and its offices thin. Business that once moved through proper channels now moves through whoever happens to be willing, and the difference — between a ship governed and a ship merely running — has begun to show at the edges, in the small matters that fall through and are not caught.

Somewhere in the last stretch the government stopped quite working. Not dramatically, no coup and no collapse, but the quorums come harder and the appointments go unmade, and more and more the ship is held together by habit and goodwill where once it was held together by its institutions. The decks feel the slack, and are uneasy in it.
stability_firming: The ship's government has quietly found its feet again. The councils meet and reach quorum, the offices are filled and answer for their work, and the charter is honored not as an old custom but as a working rule — the unshowy machinery of a polity that has decided, once more, to be governed.

It shows in the small things first: a decision made cleanly through proper channels, an office filled the day it falls vacant, a dispute settled by the standing law rather than by whoever shouts loudest. The institutions are firm again, and the ship moves with the quiet confidence of a place where the rules mean what they say.

Something has been set right in the ship's governance of late. The business of the vessel moves through its proper offices again, the councils work, and there is across the decks the particular ease of a people who know that if a thing needs deciding, there is someone whose job it is to decide it — and who will.

The government works this season in the way that asks no notice. Quorums are reached, offices answer, the charter is kept, and the ship carries the unremarkable and invaluable steadiness of a polity whose institutions are, for now, simply doing what institutions are for.
subsystem_repair: The {name} was patched back toward working order, good for another stretch of the dark.
Field crews nursed the {name} back to something like health — not pristine, but holding.
The {name} runs truer after a hard shift of repairs; the ship breathes a little easier for it.
They coaxed the {name} back from the edge with spare parts and stubbornness, the way the ship keeps itself alive.
The {name} is mended as far as hands and stores allow out here — enough to carry on, which is enough.
subsystem_training: A new cohort trained up on the {name}; the craft passes to hands that will outlast the teachers.
The {name}'s know-how was drilled into a fresh cohort — one more link in a chain the founders started.
Young hands took their lessons on the {name} and came away able; the knowledge lives another generation.
The ship schooled a new cohort in the {name} against the day the last who understood it is gone.
ambient: A generation that has never seen a planet paints one, wrong and beautiful, across a cargo-bay wall.
The corridors are quiet. Somewhere a child is taught the names of stars none aboard will ever reach.
Maintenance crews find graffiti from a hand three generations dead and leave it untouched.
The long watches pass. The hum of the reactor has become the sound the ship calls silence.
An old song surfaces in a new dialect, its meaning drifted, its tune intact.
The gardens turn another slow season. Someone marks a harvest festival no calendar remembers ordering.
A keeper updates the founding log and pauses, unsure which parts are still true.
Two children race the length of a deck their great-grandparents surveyed, and win against nothing but time.
The stars outside have rearranged by degrees no living eye can catch. The ship notes it and flies on.
A quiet year. The ledgers balance, the air holds, and the voyage simply continues, as it has and will.
ambient_drifted: A child asks what a planet felt like underfoot, and no one aboard can answer from memory — only from the archive, which is not the same thing.
The founders are spoken of the way old worlds speak of gods: real once, certainly, and no longer quite believed in.
A festival runs its full solemn course. No one living knows what it first commemorated, and no one thinks the worse of it for that.
The founding log is read aloud on the appointed day in a tongue the founders would need a translator to follow.
Two keepers argue, gently and for the hundredth time, over which of the old rites were carried from home and which the ship invented and forgot inventing.
The corridors are quiet. Somewhere a child is taught the names of stars in words the star-namers never used.
A hand three generations dead left instructions the ship still follows, for reasons the ship no longer remembers and has stopped needing to.
ambient_hollow: The corridor lights come up deck by deck as someone walks through, and go dark again behind them. There is no one else to keep them lit for.
A mess hall built to seat six hundred serves its forty in one warm corner, the rest of the tables stacked and sheeted like furniture in a closed house.
Footsteps carry a long way now. The ship has learned to sound half-empty, because it is, and the crew has stopped noticing the echo.
A whole residential ring stands sealed and cold, its hatches labelled with the names of families no longer numerous enough to fill it.
The muster is read in full in under a minute. It used to take the better part of an hour, and the older hands still pause where the long-gone names would have fallen.
Children play in corridors meant for crowds, their games grown large to fill the space, echoing where a thousand once made ordinary noise.
Someone counts the lit windows along the spine on a slow watch and stops counting, because the dark ones outnumber them now and there is no comfort in the tally.
ambient_lean: The mess line moves fast now: the ration is measured, the plate is small, and there is no second helping to slow anyone down. Everyone has learned not to look at the serving spoon too hopefully.
The grow-decks run at a hard, joyless pitch, every lamp burning and every bed doubled, coaxing calories out of soil that is asked for more than it can easily give.
Children are taught to clean the plate to the glaze and to name it a virtue. Their grandparents remember when it was only manners.
The talk in the corridors circles back, always, to the harvest — the yield figures traded like weather, the good beds and the failing ones known to everyone by name.
A festival comes and the feast is a gesture: a slightly fuller plate, a sweet no one has tasted in a year, and the ache of pretending, for an evening, that the larder is not what it is.
The old recipes are pared down and down again, the ship's cooks grown expert at the alchemy of making little seem like enough, and the crew expert at believing them.
No one is starving, quite. But the hunger has moved in — a low, constant tenant behind the day's work — and the ship has stopped expecting it to leave.
ambient_fat: The mess line is a slow, easy thing these days — second helpings offered without a second thought, the good bread every day and not just on the feast, nobody watching the serving spoon.
The grow-decks run at a comfortable idle, half the beds fallow by choice, the gardeners with time to breed a sweeter fruit rather than merely a hardier one.
A festival comes and the feast is simply a feast — no rationing dressed as celebration, no ache under the plenty, just a ship that can afford to be generous with itself for an evening.
The children here are round-cheeked and loud and take the full larder as the plain shape of the world, which, for the whole of their lives, it has been.
The old hands who remember leaner decades keep a wary corner of the granary sealed against a rainy century, and let the rest of the ship enjoy not knowing it is there.
There is a particular ease to the corridors of a well-fed ship — quarrels that stay small, favors given freely, a hundred small kindnesses that hunger would have quietly priced out.
The ledgers are fat, the stores are fat, the year is fat, and for once the loudest thing on the ship is laughter and not the low arithmetic of getting by.

travel: Departure burn complete — the ship is underway.
The drives light again — the ship burns onward toward the next waypoint.
Underway once more, deeper into the dark than any chart aboard reaches.
operation: The ship makes station. On-site operations begin.
The ship makes station again, at the second site — the crews return to the work.
On station once more; the long labor resumes where the last generation left it.
return: Objective work concluded — course set for home.
The work is done at last — the long course for home is laid in.
completion: The ship returns to its home berth.

complete: After {years} years and {generation} generations, the ship came home to its berth — the charter met in full, the manifest closed, the founders' promise kept by hands that never knew them.

The ship made her home berth on schedule and on her word, {years} years out and whole. The generation that berthed her had heard of the launch the way others hear of scripture, and they brought her home entire.
partial: The ship came home after {years} years with most of what she went for — not the whole of the charter, but enough to berth with heads up and holds not empty.
Twenty-odd decades out and back, the ship made berth having done the greater part of what was asked. The ledger was short a line or two; the crew that tied her up were not ashamed of it.
pyrrhic: The ship limped into her berth after {years} years, the charter half-kept and the cost written in the thinned decks and the quiet at the tables. She came home. Some voyages, that is the whole of the victory.
After {years} bitter years the ship made berth, less than she set out to do and fewer than she set out with. They tied up in silence and were glad, mostly, to be tied up at all.
failure: The ship returned after {years} years with the charter unmet — a voyage argued over for generations by people who had not been born when she launched, and were not believed when they said how far she had gone for so little.
{generation} generations spent, and the ship came home having failed the writ she carried. There was pay owed to none of it. There was, at least, a home berth, and a ship to tie to it.
